algorithms.name: Merge Sort
algorithms.analogy: Split a deck of cards in half, sort each half, then merge both halves back by picking the smaller card each time.
algorithms.story: Imagine you have 8 employees and need to rank them by performance score. You split them into groups of 2, rank each pair, then merge the pairs into groups of 4, and finally merge into one ranked list of 8. Each merge step takes O(n) — and you do log(n) levels of merging. Merge sort is the algorithmic version of this divide-and-conquer intuition.
algorithms.whenToUse:
• You need guaranteed O(n log n) worst case (Quick Sort degrades to O(n²))
• Sorting stability matters (equal elements keep original order)
• Sorting a linked list (merge sort works naturally; quick sort needs random access)
• External sorting (data too large for memory — merge chunks from disk)
• You need a divide-and-conquer teaching example
algorithms.howItWorks:
1. Base case: if array has 0 or 1 elements, it's already sorted — return
2. Find the midpoint: mid = (left + right) / 2
3. Recursively sort the left half: mergeSort(arr, left, mid)
4. Recursively sort the right half: mergeSort(arr, mid+1, right)
5. Merge the two sorted halves:
   a. Create a temp array
   b. Use two pointers (i for left, j for right)
   c. Each step, pick the smaller of arr[i] and arr[j] into temp
   d. Copy remaining elements from whichever half isn't exhausted
6. Copy temp back into arr[left..right]
algorithms.interviewTips:
• Explain the recurrence: T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n), which solves to O(n log n) via Master Theorem
• Stability is a key differentiator — merge sort is stable, quick sort is not (in its typical implementation)
• Space complexity O(n) is the main weakness — mention bottom-up merge sort to reduce stack space
• Java's Arrays.sort() for objects uses TimSort — a hybrid of merge sort and insertion sort
algorithms.useCases: [{"title":"Java's TimSort","desc":"Java's Arrays.sort() for Object arrays uses TimSort, a hybrid of merge sort and insertion sort that exploits existing sorted runs for performance."},{"title":"External Sort","desc":"Databases sorting data larger than RAM split data into sorted chunks on disk and use k-way merge to produce the final sorted output."},{"title":"Inversion Count","desc":"Counting inversions in an array (how far from sorted it is) is solved by modifying the merge step to count cross-half comparisons."}]
algorithms.pitfalls: ["Forgetting the base case: without left >= right check, the recursion never terminates","Wrong merge condition: using arr[i] < arr[j] instead of arr[i] <= arr[j] breaks stability","Allocating temp array inside the recursive call: creates O(n log n) total allocations — allocate once at top level for performance","Off-by-one in merge: confusing mid vs mid+1 for the start of the right half"]
algorithms.variants: [{"name":"Bottom-Up Merge Sort","desc":"Iterative version that avoids recursion stack — merges subarrays of size 1, then 2, then 4, etc."},{"name":"Natural Merge Sort","desc":"Detects existing sorted runs and uses them as the base units, making it O(n) on nearly-sorted data"}]

algorithms.name: Quick Sort
algorithms.analogy: Pick a soldier as sergeant, move all weaker soldiers to the left and stronger ones to the right, then repeat in each group.
algorithms.story: Think of organising a race. You pick one runner as the 'pivot'. Everyone slower moves behind them, everyone faster goes ahead. Now you have two groups and repeat the process in each group. The whole race is sorted after log(n) rounds of this. Quick Sort does exactly this — but with numbers. The key insight is that after partitioning, the pivot is always in its final position.
algorithms.whenToUse:
• In-place sorting needed (O(1) extra space, ignoring stack)
• Average case performance matters more than worst case
• Sorting primitive arrays (Java uses dual-pivot quick sort for int[], long[])
• Cache performance matters — in-place operations are cache-friendly
• You can choose a good pivot (random or median-of-three)
algorithms.howItWorks:
1. Base case: if array has 0 or 1 elements, return
2. Choose a pivot element (last element, random, or median-of-three)
3. Partition: rearrange elements so all < pivot are left, all > pivot are right
   a. Use two pointers: i starts before left, j scans from left to right-1
   b. When arr[j] <= pivot, swap arr[++i] with arr[j]
   c. After scanning, swap arr[i+1] with pivot to place pivot in final position
4. Recursively sort left partition: quickSort(arr, left, pivotIndex - 1)
5. Recursively sort right partition: quickSort(arr, pivotIndex + 1, right)
algorithms.interviewTips:
• Always mention the O(n²) worst case on sorted/reverse-sorted input — and how randomized pivot selection fixes it
• Lomuto vs Hoare partition: Hoare is more efficient (fewer swaps) but harder to implement — mention both
• Java's Arrays.sort() for primitive arrays uses dual-pivot quick sort (not merge sort) because it's faster in practice
• For interviews, 3-way partitioning (Dutch National Flag) handles arrays with many duplicate elements
algorithms.useCases: [{"title":"Java Arrays.sort() for Primitives","desc":"Java uses dual-pivot QuickSort for int[], long[], double[] arrays — it's faster than merge sort in practice due to cache locality and in-place sorting."},{"title":"Order Statistics","desc":"The QuickSelect algorithm (partition without recursing into both halves) finds the k-th smallest element in O(n) average time."},{"title":"Database Query Execution","desc":"PostgreSQL's sort node uses a variant of quick sort for in-memory sorts when the data fits in work_mem."}]
algorithms.pitfalls: ["Always picking first/last element as pivot on sorted input causes O(n²) — use random pivot or median-of-three","Stack overflow on large inputs with bad pivot choices — iterative implementation or tail-call optimization avoids this","Not handling duplicates: Lomuto partition on all-equal arrays degrades to O(n²) — use 3-way partition","Off-by-one in partition: the loop boundary and swap position must be precise or elements are misplaced"]
algorithms.variants: [{"name":"Dual-Pivot QuickSort","desc":"Uses two pivots instead of one, creating three partitions — used by Java's Arrays.sort() for better cache performance"},{"name":"QuickSelect","desc":"Only recurse into the partition containing the k-th element — finds k-th smallest in O(n) average time without full sort"}]

algorithms.name: Bubble Sort
algorithms.analogy: Heavy bubbles sink to the bottom — the largest element 'bubbles up' to its correct position each pass.
algorithms.story: Imagine arranging students in height order by only comparing neighbours. You walk along the line, and whenever two adjacent students are out of order, they swap. After one full pass, the tallest student is at the end. After n passes, the line is sorted. Bubble Sort does exactly this — simple, slow, but a great foundation for understanding why smarter algorithms like Merge Sort win.
algorithms.whenToUse:
• Teaching sorting concepts — the swapping logic is easy to visualise
• Array is nearly sorted (with early-exit optimisation, O(n) best case)
• Very small arrays where simplicity matters more than performance
• When in-place and stable sort is needed AND performance is not a concern
algorithms.howItWorks:
1. Repeat n-1 times (outer loop, pass = 0 to n-2)
2. In each pass, compare adjacent pairs (inner loop, i = 0 to n-2-pass)
3. If arr[i] > arr[i+1], swap them
4. After each pass, the largest unsorted element is at its correct position
5. Optimisation: if no swaps occurred in a pass, array is sorted — break early
algorithms.interviewTips:
• Never suggest bubble sort as the answer — always follow up with 'in practice we'd use merge sort or Arrays.sort()'
• The early-exit optimisation is what makes best case O(n) — mention it to show you know the full picture
• Bubble sort is stable because we only swap when arr[i] > arr[i+1] (strict greater — equals don't swap)
algorithms.useCases: [{"title":"Educational Visualisations","desc":"Bubble sort is universally used to teach sorting because each swap is visible and intuitive — sorting animation tools almost always start with it."},{"title":"Nearly Sorted Data","desc":"With the early-exit flag, bubble sort detects an already-sorted array in O(n). For data that changes only slightly between sorts, this can beat O(n log n) algorithms."}]
algorithms.pitfalls: ["Forgetting the early-exit optimisation — without it, bubble sort always runs O(n²) even on sorted input","Inner loop bound: using i < n-1 instead of i < n-1-pass performs unnecessary comparisons on already-placed elements","Using bubble sort on large datasets in production code"]
algorithms.variants: [{"name":"Cocktail Shaker Sort","desc":"Bidirectional bubble sort that alternates direction each pass — reduces the 'turtle problem' where small elements at the end take many passes to reach the front"}]
